retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Muscle Energy -- Mechanism -- Crossed Extensor Reflex
retrieved_docs.content: MET uses the concept of crossed extensor reflex in the extremities when muscle damage occurs. Voluntary contraction will inhibit the same contralateral muscle and activate the contralateral antagonist muscle. [38] An example of the reflex is if one flexes their quads to lift their legs due to stepping on a nail, and the contralateral hamstring muscle contracts to help stabilize. During the signaling pathway, the efferent nerves will communicate with multiple interneurons at the level of the spinal cord, where one will relay the message to the contralateral agonist muscle to relax.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: First described in 1875 by Wilhelm Heinrich Erb and Carl Friedrich Otto Westphal, the deep tendon reflex (DTR) is essential in examining and diagnosing neurologic disease. [1] Deep tendon reflexes or, more accurately, the 'muscle stretch reflex' can aid in evaluating neurologic disease affecting afferent nerves, spinal cord synaptic connections, motor nerves, and descending motor pathways. Proper technique and interpretation of results are crucial in achieving a proper distinction between upper and lower motor neuron pathologic processes such as multiple sclerosis (MS), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), spinal cord injuries, and spinal muscular atrophies, with the presence of hyporeflexia or hyperreflexia considered a 'hard sign' of neurologic dysfunction. There are five primary deep tendon reflexes: biceps, brachioradialis, triceps, patellar, and ankle.

retrieved_docs.title: Brachioradialis Reflex -- Definition/Introduction -- Muscle Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: Anatomical variations exist within the muscle; however, there have been studies to determine the most typical presentations of motor units. One study showed that motor units receive innervation by a mean of 4 distinct endplate zones separated by a minimum of 15 mm and a maximum of 55 mm along the proximal-distal axis. Further study showed that terminal waves were present in distally innervated motor units but not in proximally innervated motor units. This finding indicates that distal motor units have tendinous termination compared to the intra-fascicular termination of the proximal motor units. These 2 findings point to the brachioradialis muscle having a series-fibered architecture composed of overlapping bands of muscle fibers. This organization of the muscle fibers determines the biomechanical action of the brachioradialis muscle along with its force-generating capacity and neural control. One postulation for the series-fibered architecture observed in the brachioradialis muscle is the allowance for significant mechanical exertion while preserving short enough muscle fibers for the effective coupling of electrical and mechanical forces. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Withdrawal Response -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, a sensory neuron activates an interneuron that decussates and crosses the spinal cord's midline, inhibiting the motor neuron that usually activates the opposing extensor muscle in the limb. This keeps the extensor muscle from counteracting the attempt of the flexor muscle to pull the limb away. This interneuron synapses and excites a somatic motor neuron that stimulates the contralateral extensor muscles. This is sometimes called the crossed-extension reflex, and it is enacted for postural support. If, for example, you are withdrawing your foot from a painful stimulus, the other leg needs to be prepared to hold your weight. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: What is 'normal' typically depends on the patient's history and past documented reflex grade. Abnormality is suggested when asymmetric reflexes are found. Once the examiner obtains a reflex on one side, they should test the same reflex on the opposite side for comparison.

retrieved_docs.title: Westphal Sign -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Observation of a hyperactive patellar tendon reflex, on the other hand, generally suggests pathology to the CNS above the L2-L4 spinal cord level, where the patellar tendon reflex is mediated. The cerebral cortex and regions of the brainstem influence the deep tendon reflexes, such as the patellar tendon reflex. [9] These areas of the brain send connections to the reflex arc via gamma motoneurons, the corticospinal tract, and likely other descending pathways of the spinal cord. [1] [9] Insight into the location of the CNS lesion is obtainable by observing deep tendon reflexes in multiple parts of the body. For example, a hyperactive patellar tendon reflex with a normal biceps reflex would suggest pathology in the spinal cord between C5/C6 (where the biceps reflex is mediated) and L2/L4 (where the patellar tendon reflex is mediated). [9] [10] Analysis of the same reflex on both sides is also helpful. For example, a hyperactive patellar tendon reflex in the right leg but a normal patellar tendon reflex in the left leg might suggest lateral corticospinal tract pathology on the right side. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord Myotatic Reflex -- Structure and Function -- Nerves of Reflexes Commonly Encountered Clinically
retrieved_docs.content: The extensor digitorum reflex also called the Braunecker-Effenberg reflex , is elicited by inducing rapid stretch in the common extensor digitorum tendon of the forearm, sending an afferent action potential to the spinal cord via the radial/posterior interosseous nerves and dorsal nerve roots C6-C7. A post-synaptic efferent action potential returns via ventral nerve roots C6-C7 to the same muscle group, provoking a reflexive muscle contraction of the extensor digitorum. An action potential is inhibited in the ventral nerve roots C8-T1, allowing for relaxation of the antagonist muscle group, the flexor digitorum profundus.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: While a bilateral absent ankle jerk usually indicates peripheral neuropathy, cauda equina syndrome can also elicit this finding. [13] Specific peripheral nerve injuries can also lead to decreased or absent DTRs. A musculocutaneous nerve injury can affect the biceps reflex, and a radial nerve injury can affect the triceps or brachioradialis reflex, depending on the anatomical area of damage in the nerve. Femoral nerve lesions can affect the patellar reflex, and tibial nerve lesions can affect the ankle reflex.

retrieved_docs.title: Brachioradialis Reflex -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As both the biceps brachii and the brachioradialis muscle receive innervation from C5 and C6 and work to flex the elbow, studies have been conducted to separate the effects of their respective reflexes. One study used an automated system to create controlled passive movement stretch reflexes in patients with upper motor lesions. Analysis of the results showed that the brachioradialis has an earlier and larger stretch response. [16] This is important in being able to differentiate the responses. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Westphal Sign -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: To understand how an abnormal patellar tendon reflex could indicate a potential neurological disease, it is first useful to review the basic physiology of the patellar tendon reflex. The patellar tendon reflex is elicited by stimulating the patella tendon, classically by a reflex hammer, located inferior to the patella. The stimulation stretches muscle spindles located in the quadriceps muscles, and this muscle spindle stretch excites afferent sensory neurons that innervate the muscle spindles. These afferent sensory neurons then synapse on alpha motor neurons located in the anterior horn area of the spinal cord. [9] The alpha motor neurons then stimulate the quadriceps muscle to contract, producing the reflex response of the quadriceps muscle. The reflex is mediated by the femoral nerve, originating at the L2-L4 level of the spinal cord. It is a monosynaptic reflex because only one synapse produces the reflex response between the afferent sensory neuron and the efferent motor neuron. [10] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Achilles Reflex -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Examining the DTRs such as the Achilles reflex provides insight into the function of the reflex arc and its' symmetry or lack thereof when compared to the contralateral side. Deep tendon reflexes remain an essential component of the neurologic exam that can guide further clinical decisions and differentiate between upper motor neuron and lower motor neuron pathology. [2] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Upper Motor Nerve Signs -- Clinical Significance -- Co-contraction
retrieved_docs.content: Physiologic co-contraction is the concomitant contraction of agonist and antagonist muscle groups in preparation for a movement or as a response to environmental stimuli to maintain adequate tension around a joint. A pathologic co-contraction as a UMN sign is a hyperactive stretch reflex in an antagonist muscle generated in response to normal contraction of an agonistic muscle, which results in decreased rates of rapid alternating movement. There is ultimately greater fatigability and decreased ability to perform voluntary movements. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Introduction -- Triceps Reflex
retrieved_docs.content: Muscle involved: triceps brachii Nerve supply: radial Segmental innervation: C7-C8

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord Myotatic Reflex -- Structure and Function -- Nerves of Reflexes Commonly Encountered Clinically
retrieved_docs.content: The quadriceps femoris reflex, also called the patellar reflex , is elicited by inducing rapid stretch in the common quadriceps tendon distal to the patella (technically the patellar ligament, but in this functional context, the quadriceps femoris tendon), sending an afferent action potential to the spinal cord via the femoral nerve and dorsal nerve roots L2-L4, (primarily L4). A post-synaptic efferent action potential returns via ventral nerve roots L2-L4 to the same muscle group, provoking a reflexive muscle contraction of the quadriceps. An action potential is inhibited in the ventral nerve roots L5-S1, allowing for relaxation of the antagonist muscle group, the hamstring muscle group.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Introduction -- Brachioradialis Reflex
retrieved_docs.content: Muscle involved: brachioradialis Nerve supply: radial Segmental innervation: C5-C6

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Myelopathy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Crossed radial reflex: This includes the contraction of both the biceps and wrist following a biceps reflex test.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2390
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a diagnostic category that comprises a diverse group of neuropathic and inflammatory conditions. Within this category are the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد الكحولي (multifocal alcoholic neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة (severe neuropathy), اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين (chronic multifocal inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد (multifocal mononeuritis), الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflexive atrophic changes of the lower limb), and وهن عضلي وبيل (muscular and peripheral weakness). Together, these conditions illustrate the breadth of disorders classified under other nervous system diseases, highlighting variations in etiology, severity, and anatomical distribution that are important for accurate diagnosis and targeted patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2582
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of the nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that includes a variety of specific neuropathic conditions. Within this category are “تكزز,” diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), multiple neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), severe neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with root involvement (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), mononeuritis multiplex (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد), and lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي). By classifying these diverse entities under the umbrella of “Other disorders of nervous system,” clinicians can recognize that they share a common neuro‑anatomical focus while differing in etiology, severity, and clinical presentation, which aids in systematic evaluation, differential diagnosis, and tailored patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 831
retrieved_docs.content: central role of peripheral nerves in the pathophysiology of diverse neurologic disorders and underscore the need for precise classification, thorough neurologic evaluation, and targeted management to guide diagnosis, treatment, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2487
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of the nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a variety of specific neuropathic and neuromuscular conditions. Within this category are entities such as تكزز, alcoholic‑related multifocal neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد الكحولي), multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with root involvement (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), mononeuritis (التهاب العصب الأحادي) and its multifocal form (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد), distal lower‑limb reflexive sclerosis (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي), and muscular and peripheral nerve weakness (وهن عضلي وبيل). Together, these conditions illustrate the heterogeneous nature of nervous system disorders, ranging from inflammatory and autoimmune neuropathies to metabolic and degenerative processes, and highlight the importance of precise classification for accurate diagnosis and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2758
retrieved_docs.content: Baclofen, a GABA‑B receptor agonist commonly employed as a muscle relaxant, may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis. In patients with multiple sclerosis, baclofen can help alleviate spasticity and muscle stiffness, which are frequent neurological manifestations of the disease. By reducing excessive neuronal firing in the spinal cord, baclofen contributes to improved motor function and comfort, making it a valuable therapeutic option for managing the motor symptoms associated with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Hemiplegia and hemiparesis are clinical conditions that fall under the broader category of paralysis; they involve loss of motor function on one side of the body, with hemiplegia denoting complete weakness and hemiparesis indicating partial weakness. Recognizing these disorders as subtypes of paralysis helps clinicians diagnose the extent of neurological impairment and tailor rehabilitation and treatment strategies accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2228
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a diverse group of conditions, including the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب, اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث, اعتلال عصبي عضلي, التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي, الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث, and متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية. Each of these entities is classified under Other disorders of nervous system, reflecting their shared involvement of the nervous system despite differing pathophysiological mechanisms. For example, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب and اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث represent distinct forms of neuropathy, while اعتلال عصبي عضلي denotes a neuromuscular disorder. Inflammatory respiratory involvement is captured by التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي, whereas الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي describes a reflexive degenerative process affecting the lower limb. Dاء شاركو‑ماري‑توث refers to a specific neurodegenerative disease, and متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية characterizes a syndrome combining spinal cord injury–related motor deficits with neurogenic bladder dysfunction. Recognizing that all these conditions are grouped under Other disorders of nervous system aids clinicians in systematic evaluation, differential diagnosis, and coordinated management of patients presenting with complex neurological presentations.

retrieved_docs.content: strategies in the management of neuromuscular, neuro‑vascular and entrapment neuropathies, supporting precise diagnosis, coding and coordinated patient care across overlapping neurologic, sensory and musculoskeletal domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2363
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that includes a variety of specific neuropathic and neurological conditions. Within this category, the entity تكزز is classified as one of the disorders. Diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري) is also grouped under other disorders of nervous system, reflecting its role as a metabolic‑related peripheral nerve disease. Idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب) is similarly classified, indicating a neuropathy of unknown origin that worsens over time. Multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني) is included, highlighting the link between systemic autoimmune vasculitis and peripheral nerve involvement. Severe neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة) is another subclass, denoting a form of nerve disease with high clinical severity. Mononeuritis (التهاب العصب الأحادي) is also placed in this group, representing inflammation of a single peripheral nerve. Distal lower‑limb reflexive sclerosis (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي) is classified here as a localized sclerotic process affecting the lower extremities. Finally, the horse‑tail syndrome with neurogenic bladder (متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية) is included, describing a neuro‑urological syndrome that combines a characteristic tail‑like neurological presentation with bladder dysfunction. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse range of conditions encompassed by the classification “Other disorders of nervous system,” emphasizing the importance of recognizing each specific disorder for accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: pain (ألم), phantom‑limb syndrome (متلازمة الطرف الشبحي), sensory deficits, motor weakness, and paralysis (شلل). This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” serves as an umbrella classification for a diverse group of neuropathic conditions. It includes polyneuropathy that results from medication use, multiple‑nerve‑bundle neuropathies that occur in malignant diseases, and similar multifocal neuropathies associated with collagen‑vascular disorders. Severe or high‑risk neuropathies are also placed within this group, as are chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathies that involve both nerves and roots and lead to demyelination. Mononeuritis, a single‑nerve inflammation, and lower‑limb reflex sympathetic dystrophy (a painful, reflex‑driven condition of the extremities) are likewise classified under “Other disorders of nervous system.” Additionally, the term “تكزز” is listed as a subclass within this broader category, reflecting the inclusion of various language‑specific descriptors for these neurological disorders. Together, these classifications highlight the wide spectrum of peripheral and central nervous system pathologies that are grouped under “Other disorders of nervous system,” aiding clinicians in diagnosis, coding, and management of these heterogeneous conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that is classified as تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب السكري, اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني, اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة, اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين, التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد, الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, and وهن عضلي وبيل. These classifications encompass a range of neuropathic conditions, including diabetic neuropathy, multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease, severe neuropathy, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, mononeuritis multiplex, reflexive sclerosis of the lower limb, and muscular and peripheral weakness. Recognizing that these diverse entities fall under the umbrella of other disorders of the nervous system aids clinicians in organizing differential diagnoses, guiding appropriate investigations, and tailoring management strategies for patients presenting with complex neurological symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” comprises a diverse group of neurological conditions, including the entity labeled تكزز, the idiopathic progressive neuropathy termed اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب, the multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease known as اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني, the chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy described as اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين, the multifocal mononeuritis referred to as التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد, the distal lower‑extremity reflexive sclerosis called الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, the horse‑tail syndrome with neurogenic bladder identified as متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية, and the muscular and bulbar weakness condition termed وهن عضلي وبيل. Together, these entities illustrate the breadth of disorders classified under “Other disorders of nervous system,” highlighting the variety of pathophysiological mechanisms and clinical presentations that fall within this diagnostic category.

This interconnected framework supports comprehensive documentation, precise diagnosis, and interdisciplinary communication for patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Dantrolen and baklofen are classified as centrally acting muscle relaxants, a group of agents that reduce skeletal muscle tone by acting on the central nervous system. These medications are used to manage conditions characterized by excessive muscle contraction, such as spasticity and certain movement disorders, and their shared mechanism of central action underlies their therapeutic role in alleviating muscle-related symptoms.
